def: A central storage location holding a project's source code and its version history.
ex: She cloned the {{repository}} to her laptop before starting the new feature.

def: A recorded snapshot of changes saved to a version control repository.
ex: Each {{commit}} should include a clear message describing the change.

def: An independent line of development that diverges from the main codebase.
ex: Create a feature {{branch}} so your work stays isolated from production.

def: To combine changes from one branch into another within version control.
ex: We will {{merge}} the release branch once all tests pass.

def: A proposal to merge code changes, opened for review before integration.
ex: He opened a {{pull request}} and tagged two reviewers for feedback.

def: A system that tracks and manages changes to source code over time.
ex: Git is the most widely used {{version control}} system today.

def: A program that translates source code into machine-executable code before running.
ex: The {{compiler}} flagged a type mismatch on line forty-two.

def: A program that executes source code directly, line by line, at runtime.
ex: Python's {{interpreter}} runs each statement without a separate compilation step.

def: The period when a program is executing, or the environment supporting it.
ex: The error surfaced only at {{runtime}}, not during compilation.

def: The process of locating and fixing defects in software code.
ex: Careful {{debugging}} revealed that the loop never terminated.

def: To restructure existing code without changing its external behaviour.
ex: We should {{refactor}} this method before adding new functionality.

def: To release and install software into a running production environment.
ex: We {{deploy}} the new build to production every Friday afternoon.

def: A reusable software structure providing conventions and tools for building applications.
ex: They built the web application on a modern JavaScript {{framework}}.

def: A collection of prewritten code that programs can call for common tasks.
ex: He imported a graphing {{library}} to render the dashboard charts.

def: An external library or module that a project requires to function.
ex: An outdated {{dependency}} introduced a serious security vulnerability.

def: A named block of code that performs a task and may return a value.
ex: This {{function}} takes two numbers and returns their sum.

def: A named storage location that holds a value which may change.
ex: Assign the user's age to a {{variable}} before the calculation.

def: A blueprint defining the data and behaviour of objects in programming.
ex: Define a {{class}} to represent each customer in the system.

def: A concrete instance of a class holding state and behaviour.
ex: Each {{object}} keeps its own copy of the instance variables.

def: A mechanism where a class derives properties and behaviour from another.
ex: Through {{inheritance}}, the child class reuses the parent's methods.

def: The ability of different types to be handled through a shared interface.
ex: {{Polymorphism}} lets one method behave differently for each subclass.

def: Bundling data with methods and hiding internal state from outside access.
ex: {{Encapsulation}} keeps the object's fields private and controlled.

def: Hiding complex details behind a simpler, higher-level interface.
ex: A good {{abstraction}} lets callers ignore the underlying complexity.

def: A technique where a function calls itself to solve subproblems.
ex: The factorial is elegantly computed using {{recursion}}.

def: Repeated execution of a block of code, or one such repetition.
ex: Each {{iteration}} of the loop processes one record.

def: The set of rules defining valid structure in a programming language.
ex: A missing semicolon caused a {{syntax}} error.

def: The meaning and behaviour associated with valid program constructs.
ex: The code compiles, but its {{semantics}} produce the wrong result.

def: A data type with only two values, true or false.
ex: The flag is stored as a {{boolean}} indicating whether login succeeded.

def: A numeric data type representing whole numbers without a fractional part.
ex: Store the item count as an {{integer}}, not a float.

def: A data type representing a sequence of text characters.
ex: Concatenate the first and last name into a single {{string}}.

def: An ordered collection of elements accessed by numeric index.
ex: Store the scores in an {{array}} and sort them ascending.

def: A control structure that repeats a block of code multiple times.
ex: Use a {{loop}} to iterate over every item in the list.

def: A statement that executes code only when a given condition holds.
ex: Wrap the discount logic in a {{conditional}} that checks membership.

def: An event signalling an error that disrupts normal program flow.
ex: Wrap the file read in a try block to catch any {{exception}}.

def: A last-in, first-out data structure for adding and removing elements.
ex: Push the frame onto the call {{stack}} before entering the function.

def: A first-in, first-out data structure processing elements in arrival order.
ex: Incoming jobs wait in a {{queue}} until a worker is free.

def: A variable that stores the memory address of another value.
ex: Dereferencing a null {{pointer}} crashed the program.

def: Allocated memory that is never released, gradually exhausting available resources.
ex: A {{memory leak}} made the server slow down after a few hours.

def: Automatic reclamation of memory no longer referenced by a program.
ex: {{Garbage collection}} frees developers from manual memory management.

def: A test verifying a single small piece of code in isolation.
ex: Write a {{unit test}} for each edge case of the parser.

def: A test checking that multiple components work correctly together.
ex: The {{integration test}} caught a mismatch between the API and database.

def: A practice of frequently merging and automatically testing code changes.
ex: Our {{continuous integration}} pipeline runs the full test suite on each push.

def: An iterative approach emphasising flexibility, collaboration, and incremental delivery.
ex: The team follows an {{agile}} process with two-week iterations.

def: A fixed short period during which a team completes planned work.
ex: We committed to five stories for the upcoming {{sprint}}.

def: A prioritised list of pending features, tasks, and fixes for a product.
ex: The product owner groomed the {{backlog}} before sprint planning.

def: An agile framework organising work into sprints with defined roles and ceremonies.
ex: In {{scrum}}, the team holds a short stand-up every morning.

def: The systematic examination of another developer's code before it is merged.
ex: The {{code review}} surfaced a subtle off-by-one error.

def: An informal, language-independent description of an algorithm's logic.
ex: She sketched the algorithm in {{pseudocode}} before writing any real code.

def: A way of organising and storing data for efficient access and modification.
ex: Choosing the right {{data structure}} greatly improved lookup performance.

def: A structure mapping keys to values using a hash function for fast lookup.
ex: A {{hash table}} gives near-constant time access by key.

def: A sequence of nodes where each node references the next element.
ex: Inserting into a {{linked list}} avoids shifting other elements.

def: A hierarchical structure where each node has at most two children.
ex: A balanced {{binary tree}} keeps search operations logarithmic.

def: A specific URL where an API receives and responds to requests.
ex: Send a POST request to the login {{endpoint}} with the credentials.

def: Software that sits between components to process requests or connect systems.
ex: Authentication is handled by a {{middleware}} before the route runs.

def: A small, independently deployable service focused on one business capability.
ex: Each {{microservice}} owns its own database and deploys separately.

def: An application built and deployed as a single, unified unit.
ex: They gradually split the aging {{monolith}} into smaller services.

def: The ability of a system to make progress on multiple tasks simultaneously.
ex: Proper {{concurrency}} control prevents two threads corrupting shared data.

def: The smallest unit of execution that can run independently within a process.
ex: A background {{thread}} handles logging without blocking the request.

def: Describing operations that run without blocking the main execution flow.
ex: The {{asynchronous}} call returns a promise instead of blocking.

def: A function passed to another function to run after an operation completes.
ex: Pass a {{callback}} that runs once the file has loaded.

def: A self-contained unit of code grouping related functionality together.
ex: Move the date helpers into a separate utility {{module}}.

def: A bundled collection of modules distributed and installed as one unit.
ex: Install the {{package}} from the registry with a single command.

def: The process, or output, of compiling source code into a runnable product.
ex: The nightly {{build}} failed because of a missing dependency.

def: A file produced by a build process, such as a binary or package.
ex: The pipeline uploads the compiled {{artifact}} to the registry.

def: A defect where previously working functionality breaks after a change.
ex: The update introduced a {{regression}} in the checkout flow.

def: An urgent patch released quickly to correct a critical production issue.
ex: We shipped a {{hotfix}} within an hour of the outage.

def: The future cost of choosing a quick solution over a better one.
ex: Skipping tests now will only add to our {{technical debt}}.

def: A reusable, proven solution to a common software design problem.
ex: The observer {{design pattern}} decouples publishers from subscribers.

def: The human-readable instructions written by developers before compilation.
ex: The entire {{source code}} is hosted in a private repository.

def: The region of a program where a declared variable can be accessed.
ex: The {{variable scope}} is limited to the block, so it cannot be accessed outside.

def: A classification that specifies which kind of value a variable can hold.
ex: You must declare the correct {{data type}} before assigning the value.

def: A named value that cannot be changed after it is defined.
ex: Declare PI as a {{constant}} so it never gets reassigned.

def: A symbol that performs an operation on one or more operands.
ex: The plus {{operator}} adds two numbers together in this expression.

def: A combination of values and operators that evaluates to a single value.
ex: The {{expression}} a + b * 2 evaluates to a single number.

def: A complete instruction that a program executes to perform an action.
ex: Each {{statement}} must end with a semicolon in this language.

def: A named variable in a function definition that receives an input value.
ex: Add a {{parameter}} to the function so it can accept a username.

def: The actual value passed to a function when it is called.
ex: Pass the user ID as the first {{argument}} to the function.

def: The value that a function produces and sends back to its caller.
ex: The function's {{return value}} is stored in the result variable.

def: A function that belongs to an object or class and defines behaviour.
ex: Call the toString {{method}} to convert the object into text.

def: A special method that initialises a new instance of a class.
ex: The {{constructor}} sets the initial values when the object is created.

def: A type consisting of a fixed set of named constant values.
ex: Define an {{enumeration}} to represent the days of the week.

def: An ordered, often immutable, collection of a fixed number of elements.
ex: Return a {{tuple}} containing both the name and the age.

def: A collection that maps unique keys to their associated values.
ex: Store the settings in a {{dictionary}} keyed by option name.

def: A collection of unique elements with no duplicate values allowed.
ex: Use a {{set}} to remove duplicate email addresses from the list.

def: A structure that stores key-value pairs using a hash function for lookup.
ex: A {{hash map}} gives average constant-time access to stored values.

def: A queue where each element is served according to its priority.
ex: The scheduler uses a {{priority queue}} to run urgent tasks first.

def: A tree-based structure satisfying the heap ordering property between parent and children.
ex: A binary {{heap}} lets you extract the minimum in logarithmic time.

def: A structure of nodes connected by edges representing relationships between them.
ex: Model the social network as a {{graph}} of users and connections.

def: The process of visiting every node in a tree in a specific order.
ex: In-order {{tree traversal}} visits the left subtree before the root node.

def: A traversal that explores as far as possible before backtracking.
ex: Use {{depth-first search}} to detect cycles in the dependency graph.

def: A traversal that explores all neighbours before moving to the next level.
ex: {{Breadth-first search}} finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.

def: A procedure that arranges elements of a list in a defined order.
ex: Quicksort is a fast {{sorting algorithm}} for large in-memory datasets.

def: A procedure that locates a target element within a data collection.
ex: A {{searching algorithm}} scans the array to find the matching key.

def: A measure of how running time grows as input size increases.
ex: The algorithm's {{time complexity}} is quadratic for the worst-case input.

def: A measure of how much memory an algorithm uses relative to input.
ex: Recursion increases {{space complexity}} because of the extra stack frames.

def: A notation describing the upper bound of an algorithm's growth rate.
ex: We express the loop's cost using {{big O notation}} as O(n).

def: The property of an object whose state cannot change after creation.
ex: String {{immutability}} means every edit creates a brand-new string.

def: The property of an object whose state can be modified after creation.
ex: List {{mutability}} lets you append elements without creating a new object.

def: The compiler's ability to deduce a value's type without explicit annotation.
ex: Thanks to {{type inference}}, you can omit the variable's declared type.

def: A system where variable types are checked at compile time.
ex: {{Static typing}} catches type mismatches before the program even runs.

def: A system where variable types are checked at run time.
ex: {{Dynamic typing}} lets a variable hold a string and later an integer.

def: A discipline that forbids implicit conversions between incompatible types.
ex: Under {{strong typing}}, adding a string to an integer raises an error.

def: The process of translating source code into machine or lower-level code.
ex: The {{compilation}} step converts the source files into an executable.

def: Translating source code from one high-level language into another.
ex: {{Transpilation}} converts the modern syntax into older compatible JavaScript.

def: Intermediate instructions executed by a virtual machine rather than hardware.
ex: The compiler emits {{bytecode}} that the virtual machine then interprets.

def: The process of reserving memory regions for a program's data.
ex: Dynamic {{memory allocation}} reserves space on the heap at run time.

def: An error when the call stack exceeds its available memory limit.
ex: Infinite recursion caused a {{stack overflow}} and crashed the program.

def: A pointer that references no valid memory location or object.
ex: Dereferencing a {{null pointer}} throws a fatal runtime exception.

def: A pointer referencing memory that has already been freed.
ex: Using a {{dangling pointer}} after free leads to undefined behaviour.

def: A bug where output depends on the timing of concurrent operations.
ex: A {{race condition}} corrupted the counter when two threads updated it.

def: A state where threads wait forever for each other's held resources.
ex: The two threads entered a {{deadlock}}, each holding a lock the other needed.

def: A lock ensuring only one thread accesses a resource at a time.
ex: Acquire the {{mutex}} before modifying the shared buffer.

def: A counter controlling access of multiple threads to shared resources.
ex: The {{semaphore}} limits concurrent downloads to five at a time.

def: A function that captures variables from its surrounding lexical scope.
ex: The {{closure}} remembers the counter value even after the outer function returns.

def: A function that takes or returns other functions as values.
ex: The map method is a {{higher-order function}} that accepts a callback.

def: An anonymous function defined inline without a bound name.
ex: Pass a {{lambda expression}} to filter the list by even numbers.

def: Transforming a multi-argument function into a chain of single-argument functions.
ex: {{Currying}} lets you call add(2)(3) instead of add(2, 3).

def: Converting an object into a format for storage or transmission.
ex: JSON {{serialization}} turns the object into a transmittable string.

def: Reconstructing an object from its serialized data representation.
ex: {{Deserialization}} rebuilds the user object from the received JSON payload.

def: A pattern describing sets of strings for matching and searching text.
ex: Validate the email with a {{regular expression}} before saving it.

def: Embedding expressions directly inside a string literal to build text.
ex: Use {{string interpolation}} to insert the username into the greeting.

def: An operator selecting between two values based on a condition.
ex: The {{ternary operator}} returns yes if the flag is true, else no.

def: An operation acting on individual bits of integer operands.
ex: A {{bitwise operation}} with AND masks the lower four bits.

def: A representation of real numbers using a mantissa and exponent.
ex: {{Floating point}} arithmetic introduces small rounding errors in calculations.

def: A condition when a value exceeds the maximum its type can hold.
ex: Adding one to the maximum integer caused an {{overflow}} and wrapped around.

def: A condition when a value is smaller than the type can represent.
ex: Subtracting from the smallest value produced an {{underflow}} to zero.

def: Converting a value from one data type into another explicitly.
ex: Explicit {{type casting}} converts the float into an integer.

def: An error caused by code that violates the language's grammar rules.
ex: A missing bracket produced a {{syntax error}} during compilation.

def: An error that occurs while the program is executing.
ex: Dividing by zero triggered a {{runtime error}} that halted execution.

def: A flaw where code runs but produces incorrect results.
ex: A {{logic error}} in the loop made the total off by one.

def: A report of the active function calls when an error occurred.
ex: The {{stack trace}} shows exactly which function threw the exception.

def: An element holding data and a reference to the next node.
ex: Each {{linked list node}} stores a value and a pointer forward.

def: A list where each node links to both neighbours bidirectionally.
ex: A {{doubly linked list}} lets you traverse in both directions efficiently.

def: An algorithm that finds a target by repeatedly halving a sorted range.
ex: {{Binary search}} locates the value in logarithmic time on a sorted array.

def: When two distinct keys produce the same hash value.
ex: A {{hash collision}} forces the map to store both entries in one bucket.

def: A structure tracking active function calls and their return points.
ex: Each function call pushes a new frame onto the {{call stack}}.

def: A memory region used for dynamic, run-time allocation of objects.
ex: Large objects are stored in {{heap memory}} rather than on the stack.

def: An array that automatically resizes as elements are added or removed.
ex: A {{dynamic array}} doubles its capacity when it becomes full.

def: A paradigm organizing code into objects that combine data and behavior.
ex: The team refactored the legacy scripts using {{object-oriented programming}} to model real-world entities as classes.

def: A paradigm building programs from pure functions and avoiding mutable state.
ex: By adopting {{functional programming}}, they eliminated side effects and made the data pipeline easier to test.

def: A paradigm structuring code as sequences of procedures or routines to call.
ex: The original C module followed {{procedural programming}}, with functions calling one another in a fixed order.

def: A paradigm expressing what result you want without specifying control flow.
ex: SQL is a form of {{declarative programming}}: you describe the desired data, not the retrieval steps.

def: A paradigm describing computation as explicit statements that change program state.
ex: The loop rewrites the array element by element, a classic example of {{imperative programming}}.

def: A pattern ensuring a class has only one shared instance globally.
ex: They implemented the configuration loader as a {{singleton}} so every module reads the same settings.

def: A creational pattern that delegates object creation to a dedicated method.
ex: The {{factory pattern}} lets the app create the right database driver without hardcoding its class.

def: A pattern where objects subscribe to and react to another object's changes.
ex: Using the {{observer pattern}}, the UI widgets update automatically whenever the underlying model changes.

def: Supplying an object's dependencies from outside rather than creating them internally.
ex: With {{dependency injection}}, we pass a mock repository into the service during unit tests.

def: A principle where a framework, not your code, controls the program flow.
ex: The framework applies {{inversion of control}} by calling your handlers instead of you calling it.

def: A principle keeping distinct responsibilities in separate, independent parts of code.
ex: Good {{separation of concerns}} keeps the rendering logic isolated from the data-fetching layer.

def: A principle stating each module should have exactly one reason to change.
ex: Following {{single responsibility}}, we split the class so validation and persistence live separately.

def: The degree to which one module depends on another module's internals.
ex: Tight {{coupling}} between the payment and email modules made every change risky.

def: How focused and related the responsibilities within a single module are.
ex: High {{cohesion}} means the class's methods all work toward one clear purpose.

def: Designing a system as independent, interchangeable components with clear interfaces.
ex: The plugin architecture improved {{modularity}}, letting teams ship features without touching the core.

def: A surface symptom in code hinting at a deeper design problem.
ex: A method spanning three hundred lines is a classic {{code smell}} signaling it needs refactoring.

def: Automatically releasing every passing change straight to production without manual gates.
ex: With {{continuous deployment}}, each merged pull request goes live within minutes automatically.

def: Keeping code always releasable, with the final release triggered manually.
ex: Their {{continuous delivery}} setup builds a deployable artifact on every commit, ready for one-click release.

def: An automated sequence compiling, testing, and packaging code into artifacts.
ex: The {{build pipeline}} failed because a linting step flagged unused imports before compilation.

def: A scheme numbering releases as major.minor.patch to signal compatibility impact.
ex: Following {{semantic versioning}}, we bumped the major number because the API broke backward compatibility.

def: Reverting a system to a previous stable state after a bad release.
ex: When error rates spiked, the on-call engineer triggered an immediate {{rollback}} to the prior version.

def: A toggle that enables or disables functionality without redeploying code.
ex: We hid the new checkout behind a {{feature flag}} and enabled it for five percent of users.

def: A metric showing what percentage of code your tests actually execute.
ex: The pull request was blocked because it dropped {{code coverage}} below the eighty percent threshold.

def: Writing a failing test first, then code to make it pass.
ex: Practicing {{test-driven development}}, she wrote the assertion before implementing the parser function.

def: Specifying software behavior in plain-language scenarios shared across the whole team.
ex: Using {{behavior-driven development}}, analysts wrote given-when-then scenarios that mapped directly to automated tests.

def: Replacing a dependency with a fake that verifies how it's called.
ex: By {{mocking}} the payment gateway, we tested the checkout flow without charging real cards.

def: Providing a dependency with canned responses so tests run predictably.
ex: We handled the flaky network call by {{stubbing}} the client to return a fixed JSON payload.

def: Testing a complete user flow through the whole system as deployed.
ex: The {{end-to-end testing}} suite drives a real browser from login through checkout to confirmation.

def: Measuring how a system behaves under expected or peak traffic volumes.
ex: During {{load testing}}, the API held up at ten thousand concurrent requests before latency climbed.

def: Re-running tests to confirm new changes haven't broken existing functionality.
ex: Automated {{regression testing}} caught that the refactor had silently broken the discount calculation.

def: Examining source code for defects without actually executing the program.
ex: The {{static analysis}} scan flagged a possible null dereference before the code even ran.

def: Automatically checking code for style violations and likely programming errors.
ex: {{linting}} rejected the commit because a variable was declared but never used.

def: Applying consistent indentation, spacing, and layout rules across the codebase.
ex: A pre-commit hook enforces {{code formatting}} so every file follows the same style automatically.

def: Two developers working together at one workstation on the same task.
ex: Through {{pair programming}}, the senior engineer mentored the junior while they solved the bug together.

def: When Git cannot automatically reconcile competing changes to the same lines.
ex: She resolved the {{merge conflict}} by keeping both functions and manually combining the imports.

def: Moving a branch's commits onto a new base to linearize history.
ex: Before opening the pull request, he chose to {{rebase}} his branch onto the latest main.

def: Applying a single specific commit from one branch onto another.
ex: We had to {{cherry-pick}} the hotfix commit onto the release branch without merging everything else.

def: A curated file listing notable changes for each software release.
ex: The {{changelog}} noted that version 2.1 removed the deprecated authentication endpoint.

def: Reference material describing an interface's endpoints, parameters, and expected responses.
ex: The {{API documentation}} lists every endpoint, its query parameters, and sample response payloads.

def: A web interface using HTTP verbs and resources following REST conventions.
ex: The mobile app fetches user profiles from a {{RESTful API}} using standard GET requests.

def: A query language letting clients request exactly the data fields they need.
ex: Switching to {{GraphQL}} let the frontend request only the fields it needed in one call.

def: An HTTP callback that automatically notifies a system when events occur.
ex: The payment provider sends a {{webhook}} to our server whenever a transaction succeeds.

def: Restricting how many requests a client may make within a period.
ex: The API returned 429 because {{rate limiting}} caps each key at a hundred requests per minute.

def: A credential string proving a user's identity on subsequent API requests.
ex: The client attaches the {{authentication token}} in the Authorization header of every protected request.

def: Handling the lifecycle of user sessions, including creation, storage, and expiry.
ex: Poor {{session management}} let expired sessions stay valid, creating a security hole.

def: A plan deciding what data to cache, where, and when to invalidate.
ex: Our {{caching strategy}} stores rendered pages for five minutes to cut database load dramatically.

def: A buffer that decouples producers and consumers by holding messages asynchronously.
ex: Orders are pushed onto a {{message queue}} so the billing service processes them at its own pace.

def: A design where services communicate by producing and reacting to events.
ex: In their {{event-driven architecture}}, a new signup emits an event that several services independently consume.

def: Structuring an application as loosely coupled, reusable network services.
ex: Migrating to a {{service-oriented architecture}} let teams deploy the billing service independently of the catalog.

def: A single repository holding many projects or packages together.
ex: Storing all services in one {{monorepo}} makes cross-cutting refactors a single atomic change.

def: Designing systems to stay operational with minimal downtime despite failures.
ex: For {{high availability}}, the database runs across three zones so one outage never stops service.

def: A system's ability to keep functioning correctly when components fail.
ex: Retry logic and replicas give the pipeline {{fault tolerance}} even when a node crashes mid-job.

def: A property where repeating an operation yields the same result each time.
ex: We added an idempotency key so retrying the payment request preserves {{idempotency}} and never double-charges.

def: A design where each request carries all context and stores no server state.
ex: The server's {{statelessness}} lets any instance handle any request, simplifying horizontal scaling.

def: Recording events and messages during execution for later diagnosis.
ex: Structured {{logging}} let us search every failed request by user ID within seconds.

def: Continuously observing system metrics to detect problems and trigger alerts.
ex: {{monitoring}} paged the on-call engineer the moment response times crossed the alert threshold.

def: How well you can infer a system's internal state from its outputs.
ex: Adding traces and metrics improved {{observability}}, so we could pinpoint the slow database call instantly.

def: An automated path taking code from commit through testing to production.
ex: The {{deployment pipeline}} promotes each build through staging and production only after all checks pass.

def: An agile meeting where the team selects and estimates upcoming sprint work.
ex: During {{sprint planning}}, the team committed to eight stories after estimating each in points.

def: A meeting reflecting on the past sprint to improve future work.
ex: In the {{retrospective}}, the team agreed to add more automated tests after the last outage.

def: A short feature description written from the end user's perspective.
ex: The {{user story}} read: as a shopper, I want to save items for later.

def: The conditions a feature must satisfy to be considered complete.
ex: The story's {{acceptance criteria}} required the form to reject invalid emails with a clear message.

def: Repetitive standard code needed with little or no variation.
ex: The framework generates the setup {{boilerplate}} so developers focus on business logic instead.

def: Auto-generating a project's basic structure and starter files to begin quickly.
ex: The CLI's {{scaffolding}} created the folder layout, config, and a sample component in seconds.

def: The entire collection of source code for a software project.
ex: New hires spend their first week exploring the {{codebase}} before making any changes.

def: A short note describing what a version-control commit changes and why.
ex: A clear {{commit message}} explains why the query was optimized, not just that it changed.

def: A production-like environment for final testing before releasing to users.
ex: We caught the config bug in the {{staging environment}} before it ever reached real customers.

def: The live environment where real users interact with the running software.
ex: Never test experimental migrations directly in the {{production environment}} where customers are active.

def: Solving problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results.
ex: They optimized the solution using {{dynamic programming}} to avoid recomputation.

def: An approach choosing the locally optimal option at each step.
ex: A {{greedy algorithm}} picks the best immediate choice without backtracking.

def: A strategy splitting a problem into smaller parts solved recursively.
ex: Mergesort uses {{divide and conquer}} to sort halves independently.

def: Exploring candidates and undoing choices that fail to satisfy constraints.
ex: The maze solver relies on {{backtracking}} to reverse dead-end paths.

def: Caching function results to speed up repeated calls with same inputs.
ex: Add {{memoization}} so the recursive Fibonacci runs in linear time.

def: A function mapping data of arbitrary size to fixed-size values.
ex: A good {{hash function}} distributes keys uniformly across buckets.

def: Techniques for handling two keys mapping to the same slot.
ex: Chaining is one {{collision resolution}} method for hash tables.

def: Resolving hash collisions by probing for the next empty slot.
ex: With {{open addressing}}, all entries live inside the array itself.

def: Collision handling that checks consecutive slots until an empty one.
ex: {{Linear probing}} can cause clustering when the table fills up.

def: A graph representation storing each vertex's neighbors in a list.
ex: An {{adjacency list}} is memory-efficient for sparse graphs.

def: A grid where cells indicate edges between pairs of vertices.
ex: An {{adjacency matrix}} answers edge queries in constant time.

def: Ordering a directed graph's vertices so edges point forward.
ex: A {{topological sort}} schedules tasks respecting their dependencies.

def: Finds shortest paths from a source in weighted non-negative graphs.
ex: {{Dijkstra's algorithm}} greedily expands the nearest unvisited node.

def: A complete binary tree maintaining the heap ordering property.
ex: A {{binary heap}} supports logarithmic insertion and extraction.

def: A divide-and-conquer sort partitioning around a chosen pivot element.
ex: {{Quicksort}} averages n log n but degrades on sorted input.

def: A stable divide-and-conquer sort merging recursively sorted halves.
ex: {{Mergesort}} guarantees n log n time regardless of input order.

def: Studying how resources grow with input size for algorithms.
ex: Their {{complexity analysis}} showed the loop was quadratic.

def: Average cost per operation over a worst-case sequence of operations.
ex: Dynamic array append has constant {{amortized cost}} despite resizing.

def: A condition that holds true before and after each iteration.
ex: Proving the {{loop invariant}} confirms the algorithm's correctness.

def: An unusual input at the extreme of operating parameters.
ex: Always test the empty-array {{edge case}} before shipping.

def: Trying all possibilities exhaustively without clever optimization.
ex: The {{brute force}} solution checks every pair in the array.

def: A technique maintaining a moving subrange over a sequence.
ex: Use a {{sliding window}} to find the longest substring efficiently.

def: Using two indices moving through data to solve problems.
ex: The {{two pointers}} method finds a target pair in sorted arrays.

def: Directly modifying individual bits within integer values.
ex: {{Bit manipulation}} can toggle flags stored in a single integer.

def: The reference element around which partitioning organizes other values.
ex: Choosing a random {{pivot}} avoids quicksort's worst case.

def: Rearranging elements into groups relative to a chosen boundary.
ex: The {{partition}} step places smaller values before the pivot.

def: Sorting that rearranges elements using only constant extra memory.
ex: Heapsort is an {{in-place sorting}} algorithm needing no buffer.

def: A sort preserving the relative order of equal elements.
ex: A {{stable sort}} keeps earlier ties before later ones.

def: A simple sort repeatedly swapping adjacent out-of-order elements.
ex: {{Bubble sort}} is easy to teach but quadratically slow.

def: Building a sorted list by inserting each element into place.
ex: {{Insertion sort}} performs well on nearly sorted small inputs.

def: Repeatedly selecting the minimum and moving it to the front.
ex: {{Selection sort}} always runs in quadratic time regardless of data.

def: Sorting by repeatedly extracting the maximum from a heap.
ex: {{Heapsort}} achieves n log n without extra memory.

def: A non-comparison sort tallying occurrences of each distinct key.
ex: {{Counting sort}} runs linearly when the key range is small.

def: Sorting numbers digit by digit using a stable subroutine.
ex: {{Radix sort}} orders fixed-width integers without comparisons.

def: Computing a route between nodes in a graph or grid.
ex: Game AI uses {{pathfinding}} to navigate around obstacles.

def: A best-first search guided by a heuristic estimate to goal.
ex: {{A* search}} finds optimal paths faster than uninformed methods.

def: A practical rule guiding search toward good enough solutions.
ex: The {{heuristic}} estimates remaining distance to the target.

def: A tree connecting all vertices with the least total edge weight.
ex: Kruskal's algorithm builds a {{minimum spanning tree}} greedily.

def: The route between two nodes minimizing total traversal cost.
ex: The GPS computes the {{shortest path}} to your destination.

def: A structure tracking elements partitioned into disjoint sets efficiently.
ex: {{Union-find}} detects cycles while building a spanning tree.

def: An ordered tree where left keys precede the node's key.
ex: A balanced {{binary search tree}} keeps lookups logarithmic.

def: A tree automatically restructuring to keep its height low.
ex: An AVL tree is a {{self-balancing tree}} using rotations.

def: A local restructuring operation rebalancing a binary search tree.
ex: A left {{tree rotation}} restores balance after an insertion.

def: A tree storing strings by shared character prefixes.
ex: A {{trie}} enables fast autocomplete over a dictionary.

def: Systematically visiting every reachable vertex in a graph.
ex: Both BFS and DFS perform {{graph traversal}} differently.

def: Determining whether a graph contains a closed loop.
ex: {{Cycle detection}} prevents infinite loops in dependency graphs.

def: A maximal subgraph where every vertex reaches every other.
ex: Counting each {{connected component}} reveals isolated clusters.

def: Notation describing growth rates as input size approaches infinity.
ex: {{Asymptotic notation}} hides constant factors and lower terms.

def: The maximum resource usage over all possible inputs.
ex: Quicksort's {{worst case}} occurs on already sorted data.

def: Expected resource usage averaged over typical or random inputs.
ex: The {{average case}} for hashing lookups is constant time.

def: Running time growing as a constant raised to input size.
ex: Naive subset generation takes {{exponential time}} to finish.

def: Running time growing proportionally to the logarithm of input.
ex: Balanced tree lookups run in {{logarithmic time}}.

def: Running time growing directly in proportion to input size.
ex: Scanning an array once completes in {{linear time}}.

def: Running time independent of the size of the input.
ex: Array indexing by position takes {{constant time}}.

def: Running time bounded by a polynomial in input size.
ex: Problems solvable in {{polynomial time}} are considered tractable.

def: Hardest problems in NP where any is reducible to another.
ex: The travelling salesman decision problem is {{NP-complete}}.

def: Transforming one problem into another to prove relative difficulty.
ex: A polynomial {{reduction}} maps satisfiability onto graph coloring.

def: An equation defining a term using earlier terms of a sequence.
ex: Solving the {{recurrence relation}} yields the algorithm's complexity.

def: A formula giving complexity for common divide-and-conquer recurrences.
ex: Apply the {{master theorem}} to bound the recursion quickly.

def: Bottom-up dynamic programming filling a table iteratively.
ex: {{Tabulation}} avoids recursion by building answers upward.

def: When an optimal solution builds from optimal solutions of subproblems.
ex: Shortest paths exhibit {{optimal substructure}}, enabling dynamic programming.

def: A cumulative array where each entry sums preceding elements.
ex: A {{prefix sum}} answers range queries in constant time.

def: Mapping keys to array indices for fast average lookups.
ex: {{Hashing}} gives near constant-time membership checks.

def: The ratio of stored entries to total hash table capacity.
ex: A high {{load factor}} increases collisions and slows lookups.

def: Rebuilding a hash table with a larger array size.
ex: {{Rehashing}} redistributes keys when the table grows full.

def: A double-ended queue allowing insertion and removal at both ends.
ex: A {{deque}} supports the sliding window maximum efficiently.

def: A fixed-size buffer treating its storage as a wrapped loop.
ex: A {{circular buffer}} reuses freed slots without shifting data.

def: Exploring as far along each branch before backtracking.
ex: A {{depth-first traversal}} uses a stack, explicit or recursive.

def: Visiting all neighbors at one depth before going deeper.
ex: A {{breadth-first traversal}} finds shortest unweighted paths.

def: An algorithm making random choices to influence its behavior.
ex: A {{randomized algorithm}} picks pivots to dodge worst cases.

def: An algorithm returning a near-optimal answer with a guarantee.
ex: An {{approximation algorithm}} handles hard problems within a bound.

def: Enumerating every candidate solution to guarantee finding the best.
ex: {{Exhaustive search}} is only feasible for small input sizes.

def: The study of counting, arrangement, and combination of objects.
ex: {{Combinatorics}} determines how many orderings a problem allows.

def: An arrangement of elements where order matters distinctly.
ex: Generating every {{permutation}} of a string grows factorially.

def: Producing the same output every time for identical input.
ex: A {{deterministic}} algorithm never relies on random choices.

def: Selecting the option looking best at the current moment.
ex: The {{greedy choice}} property justifies ignoring future consequences.

def: A structure supporting efficient prefix sums and point updates.
ex: A {{binary indexed tree}} updates and queries in logarithmic time.

def: A tree answering range queries and updates over an array.
ex: A {{segment tree}} computes range minimums in logarithmic time.

def: Computing gradients by propagating errors backward through a network.
ex: {{Backpropagation}} adjusts weights to reduce prediction error.

def: A verb like GET or POST indicating the action a request performs.
ex: Choose the correct {{HTTP method}} for creating a resource.

def: Metadata sent with a request describing content, authorization, or client details.
ex: Add the token to the {{request header}} before sending.

def: The main data payload returned by a server after processing a request.
ex: Parse the {{response body}} to extract user data.

def: A three-digit number indicating the outcome of an HTTP request.
ex: A 404 {{status code}} means the resource was not found.

def: A key-value pair appended to a URL after a question mark for filtering.
ex: Filter results using the page {{query parameter}}.

def: A variable segment embedded within a URL path identifying a specific resource.
ex: The user ID is a {{path parameter}} in the route.

def: Structured data formatted as JSON sent in a request or response.
ex: Send the form data as a {{JSON payload}}.

def: A browser mechanism allowing servers to permit requests from other domains.
ex: Enable {{cross-origin resource sharing}} to allow the frontend to call the API.

def: A software layer that verifies user identity before granting request access.
ex: The {{authentication middleware}} rejects requests without a valid token.

def: Obtaining a new access token using a refresh token before expiry.
ex: Implement {{token refresh}} to keep the user logged in.

def: Splitting a large result set into discrete pages returned per request.
ex: Add {{pagination}} to avoid loading thousands of records at once.

def: Limiting how frequently a function or request can be executed over time.
ex: Apply {{throttling}} to reduce load on the search endpoint.

def: A request producing the same result no matter how many times repeated.
ex: A PUT call should be an {{idempotent request}}.

def: A protocol where each request contains all information, retaining no prior context.
ex: HTTP is a {{stateless protocol}} by design.

def: Generating a page's HTML on the server before sending it to the browser.
ex: Use {{server-side rendering}} to improve initial load speed.

def: Generating page content in the browser using JavaScript after loading.
ex: {{Client-side rendering}} shifts work to the user's browser.

def: A web app that loads once and updates content dynamically without reloads.
ex: The dashboard is built as a {{single-page application}}.

def: Mapping URLs to specific handlers, components, or pages in an application.
ex: Configure {{routing}} so each URL renders the right view.

def: A tool that combines templates with data to produce final HTML output.
ex: The {{template engine}} injects variables into the page markup.

def: Checking user-submitted form data against rules before processing it.
ex: Add {{form validation}} to reject empty email fields.

def: An attack injecting malicious scripts into pages viewed by other users.
ex: Escape user input to prevent {{cross-site scripting}}.

def: An attack tricking a user's browser into sending unwanted authenticated requests.
ex: Use tokens to defend against {{cross-site request forgery}}.

def: Distributed servers delivering content from locations near each user.
ex: Serve static assets through a {{content delivery network}}.

def: A server forwarding client requests to one or more backend servers.
ex: Nginx acts as a {{reverse proxy}} in front of the app.

def: A component distributing incoming traffic across multiple backend servers.
ex: The {{load balancer}} spreads requests evenly across instances.

def: A protocol providing full-duplex communication over a single persistent connection.
ex: Use a {{WebSocket}} for real-time chat messages.

def: A technique holding a request open until the server has new data.
ex: Fall back to {{long polling}} when sockets are unavailable.

def: Selecting the best response format based on client-provided preference headers.
ex: {{Content negotiation}} lets the API return JSON or XML.

def: A standard identifier for a data format, such as application/json.
ex: Set the {{media type}} to application/json in the header.

def: Converting characters into a format safely transmittable within a URL.
ex: Apply {{URL encoding}} to special characters in the query.

def: The common root address to which relative API paths are appended.
ex: Configure the {{base URL}} once for all API calls.

def: A small piece of data stored by the browser and sent with requests.
ex: Store the session ID in a secure {{cookie}}.

def: A browser API storing key-value data persistently on the client.
ex: Save the theme preference in {{local storage}}.

def: An access credential sent in the authorization header to prove identity.
ex: Include the {{bearer token}} in every protected request.

def: A compact, signed token encoding claims for stateless authentication.
ex: The server issues a {{JSON Web Token}} after login.

def: An open standard for delegated authorization without sharing passwords.
ex: Sign in with Google using {{OAuth}}.

def: A secret string identifying and authorizing a client calling an API.
ex: Pass your {{API key}} in the request header.

def: HTTP secured with encryption to protect data in transit.
ex: Serve all pages over {{HTTPS}} to encrypt traffic.

def: The negotiation establishing encryption keys before secure communication begins.
ex: The {{TLS handshake}} adds latency to the first request.

def: Translating a domain name into its corresponding IP address.
ex: Slow {{DNS resolution}} delays the page load.

def: A human-readable, hyphenated part of a URL identifying a page.
ex: Generate a {{URL slug}} from the article title.

def: A response instructing the browser to load a different URL.
ex: Return a 301 {{redirect}} to the new page.

def: Synchronizing data between the model and the user interface automatically.
ex: Two-way {{data binding}} updates the input and model together.

def: An in-memory representation of the UI used to optimize real DOM updates.
ex: The framework diffs the {{virtual DOM}} to apply minimal changes.

def: Programmatically changing the structure or content of a web page.
ex: Use JavaScript for {{DOM manipulation}} after the page loads.

def: A function registered to run when a specific event occurs.
ex: Attach a click {{event listener}} to the button.

def: An event propagating from the target element upward through ancestors.
ex: Stop {{event bubbling}} to prevent the parent handler firing.

def: A pattern used to target specific HTML elements for styling.
ex: Write a {{CSS selector}} to style all list items.

def: Designing layouts that adapt fluidly to different screen sizes.
ex: {{Responsive design}} makes the site usable on phones.

def: The visible area of a web page within the browser window.
ex: Set the {{viewport}} meta tag for mobile scaling.

def: A CSS rule applying styles based on device characteristics like width.
ex: Use a {{media query}} to change layout below 600 pixels.

def: A small icon representing a website shown in browser tabs.
ex: Add a {{favicon}} so the tab shows your logo.

def: Deferring loading of resources until they are actually needed.
ex: Use {{lazy loading}} for images below the fold.

def: Breaking a bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand.
ex: {{Code splitting}} reduces the initial JavaScript payload.

def: A tool combining source modules into optimized files for the browser.
ex: The {{bundler}} merges all imports into one file.

def: Removing unnecessary characters from code to reduce file size.
ex: Enable {{minification}} to shrink the production bundle.

def: Eliminating unused code from the final bundle during the build.
ex: {{Tree shaking}} removes functions you never import.

def: A web app offering offline support and installability like native apps.
ex: Turn the site into a {{progressive web app}} for offline use.

def: A background script enabling caching, offline access, and push notifications.
ex: Register a {{service worker}} to cache assets offline.

def: The four basic actions: create, read, update, and delete data.
ex: The API exposes {{CRUD operations}} for user records.

def: The server-side part of an application handling logic and data.
ex: The {{backend}} processes payments and stores orders.

def: The client-side part of an application users directly interact with.
ex: The {{frontend}} renders the dashboard in the browser.

def: A cache of reusable database connections shared across requests.
ex: Configure the {{connection pool}} size for peak traffic.

def: A message a client sends to a server asking for an action.
ex: Each page view triggers several {{HTTP request}}s.

def: A message a server returns after processing a client's request.
ex: Inspect the {{HTTP response}} headers in the browser tools.

def: The data payload sent by a client within a request.
ex: Send the new user details in the {{request body}}.

def: An addressable entity like a user or order exposed by an API.
ex: Each {{resource}} has a unique URL in the API.

def: Checking that data conforms to a predefined structure and types.
ex: Run {{schema validation}} on the incoming JSON.

def: Managing changes to an API by exposing multiple numbered versions.
ex: Use {{API versioning}} so old clients keep working.

def: A single entry point routing requests to multiple backend services.
ex: The API {{gateway}} handles authentication and routing.

def: Code providing modern functionality on older browsers lacking native support.
ex: Add a {{polyfill}} so old browsers support fetch.

def: An object representing the eventual result of an asynchronous operation.
ex: The fetch call returns a {{promise}} that resolves with data.

def: A browser interface for making network requests using promises.
ex: Use the {{fetch API}} to load data from the server.

def: A technique for updating parts of a page without a full reload.
ex: Submit the form via {{AJAX}} to avoid reloading.

def: Text annotated with tags defining structure and presentation of content.
ex: The page {{markup}} uses semantic HTML tags.

def: Reducing response size using algorithms like gzip before transmission.
ex: Enable {{payload compression}} to speed up large responses.

def: Letting users authenticate once to access multiple related applications.
ex: Employees use {{single sign-on}} across all internal tools.

def: Reusing a set of open database connections instead of opening new ones each time.
ex: Enabling {{connection pooling}} cut our database latency dramatically under load.

def: Deliberately dropping requests to protect a system from overload and collapse.
ex: Under peak traffic the gateway begins {{load shedding}} low-priority requests.

def: A pattern that stops calls to a failing service to prevent cascading failures.
ex: The {{circuit breaker}} opened after five consecutive downstream timeouts.

def: A signal telling a fast producer to slow down when consumers cannot keep up.
ex: The stream applies {{backpressure}} so buffers never grow without bound.

def: A fixed group of threads or processes that pull tasks from a shared queue.
ex: We sized the {{worker pool}} to match available CPU cores.

def: A loop that waits for events and dispatches them to handlers one at a time.
ex: Blocking the {{event loop}} freezes every pending request at once.

def: Input and output operations that return immediately instead of waiting for completion.
ex: Switching to {{non-blocking IO}} let one thread serve thousands of sockets.

def: A temporary memory region holding data while it moves between two places.
ex: Data accumulates in a {{buffer}} until the socket is ready to send.

def: An endpoint for sending and receiving data across a network connection.
ex: The server opens a listening {{socket}} on port eight thousand.

def: An ordered, continuous sequence of bytes read or written without message boundaries.
ex: TCP delivers a raw {{byte stream}}, not discrete framed messages.

def: The order in which bytes of a multi-byte value are stored in memory.
ex: Network protocols fix {{endianness}} so heterogeneous machines interpret numbers alike.

def: Scattered free memory gaps that prevent allocation despite enough total space.
ex: Long-running services suffer {{heap fragmentation}} that slowly wastes usable memory.

def: Tracking how many references point to an object to know when to free it.
ex: {{Reference counting}} frees memory immediately but cannot reclaim cyclic references.

def: A lock coordinating exclusive access to a resource across multiple machines.
ex: A {{distributed lock}} ensures only one node runs the nightly job.

def: Agreement among distributed nodes on a single value despite partial failures.
ex: Raft achieves {{consensus}} by electing a leader that orders entries.

def: A guarantee that replicas converge to the same state given enough time.
ex: With {{eventual consistency}} a read may briefly return stale data.

def: A database split into partitions spread across servers to scale horizontally.
ex: Our {{sharded database}} routes each user's rows by hashing their ID.

def: A durable log recording changes before they are applied to storage.
ex: The {{write-ahead log}} lets the database recover after a sudden crash.

def: The number of copies of each data item kept across a cluster.
ex: A {{replication factor}} of three survives the loss of two nodes.

def: A managed set of reusable threads that execute submitted tasks concurrently.
ex: Submitting work to a {{thread pool}} avoids costly per-task thread creation.

def: Saving one task's state and loading another's so the CPU can multitask.
ex: Excessive threads cause frequent {{context switch}} overhead that wastes CPU.

def: A request from a program to the operating system kernel for a service.
ex: Reading a file triggers a {{system call}} into the kernel.

def: The core of an operating system managing memory, processes, and hardware access.
ex: Device drivers run inside the {{kernel}} with full privileges.

def: A running program instance with its own memory space and resources.
ex: Each {{process}} is isolated so one crash cannot corrupt another.

def: An abstraction giving each process a large private address space backed by paging.
ex: {{Virtual memory}} lets programs use more space than physical RAM.

def: Moving fixed-size memory pages between RAM and disk on demand.
ex: Heavy {{paging}} to disk slows the whole machine to a crawl.

def: An interrupt raised when a program accesses a page not in RAM.
ex: A {{page fault}} forces the kernel to load the missing page.

def: Removing or refreshing stale cached entries so reads return current data.
ex: Getting {{cache invalidation}} right is famously one of computing's hard problems.

def: A lookup successfully served from the cache instead of the origin.
ex: A high {{cache hit}} rate keeps database load pleasantly low.

def: A lookup not found in the cache, requiring a slower origin fetch.
ex: Every {{cache miss}} adds a costly round trip to storage.

def: An eviction policy discarding the entry unused for the longest time.
ex: The {{least recently used}} policy evicts stale keys before hot ones.

def: The process by which distributed nodes agree on a single coordinator.
ex: After the leader crashed, {{leader election}} promoted a new node.

def: A periodic signal a node sends to prove it is still alive.
ex: Missing three {{heartbeat}} messages marks the node as failed.

def: The minimum number of nodes that must agree for an operation to proceed.
ex: Writes commit only after a {{quorum}} of replicas acknowledges them.

def: A protocol coordinating an atomic transaction across several distributed participants.
ex: {{Two-phase commit}} blocks if the coordinator fails mid-decision.

def: A unique token letting a server safely deduplicate repeated identical requests.
ex: Retries carry the same {{idempotency key}} so payments never double-charge.

def: Stopping a service cleanly after draining in-flight work first.
ex: On deploy the pod performs a {{graceful shutdown}} before exiting.

def: A periodic probe reporting whether a service is ready to serve.
ex: The load balancer removes any pod failing its {{health check}}.

def: The amount of work or requests a system processes per unit time.
ex: Batching writes raised {{throughput}} without hurting tail latency.

def: The time taken for a single request to travel and receive a response.
ex: Adding a nearby cache slashed read {{latency}} to milliseconds.

def: The slow response times at the high percentiles of a latency distribution.
ex: A single slow shard dominates the service's {{tail latency}}.

def: An operation that halts the caller until it fully completes.
ex: A hidden {{blocking call}} inside the handler stalled the event loop.

def: A function that can suspend and resume, enabling cooperative concurrency.
ex: Each request runs in its own lightweight {{coroutine}}.

def: A small integer the kernel uses to reference an open file or socket.
ex: Leaking a {{file descriptor}} on each request eventually exhausts the limit.

def: A file whose contents are mapped directly into a process's address space.
ex: Using a {{memory-mapped file}} avoids explicit read and write calls.

def: Transferring data without copying it between kernel and user buffers.
ex: A {{zero-copy}} sendfile pipes disk data straight to the socket.

def: An operation that completes entirely or not at all, without interruption.
ex: Incrementing the counter uses an {{atomic operation}} to avoid races.

def: Slowdown caused by many threads competing for the same lock.
ex: Splitting the mutex reduced {{lock contention}} on the hot path.

def: A lock where a thread busy-waits in a loop until it acquires it.
ex: A {{spinlock}} suits very short critical sections on multicore CPUs.

def: A code region that only one thread may execute at a time.
ex: Guard the shared counter's {{critical section}} with a single mutex.

def: Threads keep responding to each other but make no actual progress.
ex: Both retrying processes entered a {{livelock}}, endlessly yielding to each other.

def: A thread perpetually denied a resource because others keep taking it.
ex: Unfair scheduling caused {{starvation}} of low-priority background jobs.

def: A Linux mechanism for efficiently monitoring many file descriptors for readiness.
ex: The server uses {{epoll}} to watch thousands of sockets cheaply.

def: The maximum time allowed to establish a connection before giving up.
ex: A short {{connection timeout}} stops threads hanging on dead hosts.

def: Retrying failed operations with progressively longer waits between attempts.
ex: Clients use {{exponential backoff}} to avoid stampeding a recovering server.

def: Random variation added to timing to spread out retries or requests.
ex: Adding {{jitter}} prevents synchronized clients from retrying in unison.

def: Many clients simultaneously hitting a resource the instant it becomes available.
ex: Cache expiry triggered a {{thundering herd}} that overwhelmed the database.

def: Keeping copies of data on multiple nodes for durability and availability.
ex: Synchronous {{data replication}} protects against a single disk failure.

def: Automatically switching to a standby system when the primary fails.
ex: Database {{failover}} promoted the replica within seconds of the outage.

def: A system's ability to keep working despite network splits between nodes.
ex: CAP forces a tradeoff once {{partition tolerance}} is required.

def: A structure tracking causal ordering of events across distributed nodes.
ex: {{Vector clock}} comparisons reveal whether two updates conflict.

def: Nodes spread state by randomly exchanging information with peers.
ex: Cluster membership propagates through a {{gossip protocol}} within seconds.

def: A compact structure testing set membership with possible false positives.
ex: A {{bloom filter}} skips disk lookups for keys certainly absent.

def: Mapping keys to nodes so adding a node moves few keys.
ex: {{Consistent hashing}} keeps most cache entries put when scaling out.

def: Placing computation near the data it needs to reduce transfer cost.
ex: Scheduling tasks for {{data locality}} avoids shuffling terabytes over the network.

def: Processing large volumes of data together in scheduled bulk jobs.
ex: Nightly {{batch processing}} recomputes every user's recommendation scores.

def: Processing data continuously as individual events arrive in real time.
ex: {{Stream processing}} flags fraudulent transactions the moment they occur.

def: Storage that keeps data as immutable objects addressed by unique keys.
ex: We archived backups to cheap {{object storage}} in the cloud.

def: A lightweight isolated package bundling an app with its dependencies.
ex: Each service ships as an immutable {{container}} image.

def: Automated coordination of deploying, scaling, and networking many containers.
ex: Kubernetes handles {{orchestration}} of hundreds of ephemeral pods.

def: Automatically adjusting the number of instances to match current demand.
ex: {{Autoscaling}} spins up extra pods during the lunchtime traffic spike.

def: Adding more machines to share load rather than enlarging one.
ex: Stateless services enable easy {{horizontal scaling}} behind a balancer.

def: Increasing a single machine's CPU, memory, or disk to handle more.
ex: {{Vertical scaling}} hits a ceiling once the biggest instance saturates.

def: Running two identical environments and switching traffic between them on release.
ex: {{Blue-green deployment}} let us roll back instantly by flipping traffic.

def: Rolling out a change to a small subset of users first.
ex: A {{canary release}} exposed the bug before it reached everyone.

def: Following a single request across many services to diagnose latency.
ex: {{Distributed tracing}} revealed the slow span buried in the auth service.

def: An infrastructure layer handling secure, observable service-to-service communication.
ex: The {{service mesh}} adds retries and mutual TLS transparently.

def: A helper container running alongside a main app to extend it.
ex: A logging {{sidecar}} ships each pod's output to central storage.

def: Middleware that routes messages between producers and consumers reliably.
ex: The {{message broker}} buffers events when consumers fall behind.

def: A messaging pattern where publishers broadcast events to many subscribers.
ex: {{Publish-subscribe}} decouples the emitter from every downstream consumer.

def: A queue holding messages that repeatedly failed processing for later inspection.
ex: Poison messages land in the {{dead letter queue}} after max retries.

def: A guarantee that each message arrives, possibly duplicated, never lost.
ex: {{At-least-once delivery}} forces consumers to handle duplicate events.

def: Letting existing connections finish before removing a server from rotation.
ex: {{Connection draining}} avoids dropping requests during a rolling deploy.

def: The most frequently executed code path where performance matters most.
ex: We removed every allocation from the request {{hot path}}.

def: Measuring where a program spends time or memory to find bottlenecks.
ex: {{Profiling}} revealed that JSON parsing dominated the CPU time.

def: Adding code to emit metrics, logs, or traces about runtime behavior.
ex: Rich {{instrumentation}} let us pinpoint the regression in minutes.

def: Numeric measurements of system behavior collected over time for analysis.
ex: Dashboards plot request {{metrics}} like error rate and latency.

def: A duration after which a cached entry or record expires.
ex: Each session token has a five-minute {{time-to-live}}.

def: The guarantee that committed data survives crashes and hardware failures.
ex: Replicating across zones raises {{data durability}} to eleven nines.

def: A consistent point-in-time copy of data or system state.
ex: A nightly {{snapshot}} lets us restore yesterday's exact dataset.

def: A saved state allowing a long job to resume after failure.
ex: The stream job writes a {{checkpoint}} every thirty seconds.

def: A service that retains client data between requests locally.
ex: A {{stateful service}} needs sticky sessions or shared storage.

def: Routing a client's requests consistently to the same backend server.
ex: A {{sticky session}} keeps the user pinned to one cache.

def: Adjusting collector parameters to balance pause times against throughput.
ex: Careful {{garbage collector tuning}} cut our pause times in half.

def: An instruction forcing an ordering constraint on memory operations.
ex: A {{memory barrier}} stops the CPU reordering these two writes.

def: Finding cycles of threads each waiting on resources the others hold.
ex: The database performs {{deadlock detection}} and aborts one victim transaction.

def: Sharing memory until a write occurs, then copying just that page.
ex: Forking a process uses {{copy-on-write}} to avoid duplicating memory eagerly.

def: A series of stages that move and transform data end to end.
ex: The {{data pipeline}} ingests logs, cleans them, and loads warehouses.

def: Applying versioned structural changes to a database over time.
ex: The {{schema migration}} added the column without downtime.

def: A guarantee that a message will arrive eventually despite temporary failures.
ex: Retry queues provide {{eventual delivery}} even during downstream outages.

def: A copy of a database serving read queries to offload the primary.
ex: Analytics queries hit a {{read replica}} to spare the primary.

def: Carrying many logical streams over a single physical connection concurrently.
ex: HTTP/2 uses {{connection multiplexing}} to send parallel requests over one socket.

def: A statement in a test that checks whether a value meets an expected condition.
ex: The failing {{assertion}} showed the function returned null instead of a list.

def: A collection of helper functions used to write readable assertions in tests.
ex: We added an {{assertion library}} so the tests read almost like plain English.

def: A fixed baseline of data or state prepared before running one or more tests.
ex: Each {{test fixture}} loads the same sample users into an in-memory database.

def: A grouped collection of test cases run together to validate a system.
ex: The full {{test suite}} takes twelve minutes to finish on the build server.

def: A single scenario with inputs and expected results that verifies one behaviour.
ex: Write a separate {{test case}} for an empty list and for a full one.

def: A tool that discovers, executes tests and reports their pass or fail results.
ex: The {{test runner}} highlighted three failures in bright red at the bottom.

def: Supporting code and tools that automate running tests and collecting outcomes.
ex: Our {{test harness}} spins up a fake server before every integration run.

def: A marked line where a debugger pauses execution so state can be inspected.
ex: Set a {{breakpoint}} on line forty and step through the loop slowly.

def: A debugger stop triggered when a specific variable or memory location changes.
ex: A {{watchpoint}} on the counter revealed exactly when it went negative.

def: A tool that runs a program step by step to find and fix defects.
ex: Attach the {{debugger}} to the process and inspect the local variables.

def: A tool that measures where a program spends time or resources.
ex: The {{profiler}} showed that sorting consumed sixty percent of the runtime.

def: A tool that tracks how much memory a program allocates and retains.
ex: The {{memory profiler}} pointed to a cache that kept growing forever.

def: The process of removing call frames while an exception propagates upward.
ex: During {{stack unwinding}} each destructor runs before control leaves the function.

def: A quick shallow test confirming the most critical features basically work.
ex: Run a {{smoke test}} after each deploy to catch obvious breakages fast.

def: A brief test verifying that results are plausible before deeper testing.
ex: A simple {{sanity check}} confirmed the totals were not obviously wrong.

def: A test that passes and fails inconsistently without any code changes.
ex: That {{flaky test}} fails once every ten runs because of a timing race.

def: A stand-in object replacing a real dependency during a test.
ex: Replace the payment gateway with a {{test double}} to avoid real charges.

def: A working but simplified implementation of a dependency used in tests.
ex: An in-memory {{fake}} stands in for the real database during unit tests.

def: A test object that records how it was called for later verification.
ex: The {{spy}} confirmed the logger was invoked exactly once with an error.

def: A test comparing current output against a stored reference snapshot.
ex: The {{snapshot test}} flagged an unexpected change in the rendered markup.

def: A technique that alters code to check whether tests detect the changes.
ex: {{mutation testing}} revealed that our tests never actually checked the discount branch.

def: Feeding random or malformed input to a program to expose crashes.
ex: A weekend of {{fuzzing}} uncovered a parser crash on truncated files.

def: Testing that checks general properties hold across many generated inputs.
ex: {{property-based testing}} generated a thousand lists to verify the sort stays ordered.

def: A test pushing a system beyond normal limits to find its breaking point.
ex: The {{stress test}} crashed the server once traffic passed ten thousand users.

def: Testing values at the edges of allowed input ranges.
ex: {{boundary testing}} caught an off-by-one error at the maximum array index.

def: Splitting inputs into groups expected to behave the same way.
ex: Using {{equivalence partitioning}}, one value per group covers the whole range.

def: Unscripted hands-on testing where the tester explores and learns simultaneously.
ex: An hour of {{exploratory testing}} surfaced bugs no scripted case had covered.

def: Observing real users to evaluate how easy a product is to use.
ex: {{usability testing}} showed users could not find the checkout button.

def: Testing based only on inputs and outputs without seeing internal code.
ex: {{black-box testing}} treats the module purely by its documented interface.

def: Testing that uses knowledge of internal code paths and structure.
ex: {{white-box testing}} let us target the rarely executed error branch directly.

def: The percentage of source lines executed at least once by the tests.
ex: Our {{line coverage}} climbed to ninety percent after adding parser tests.

def: The share of decision branches exercised by the test suite.
ex: {{branch coverage}} was low because the else path was never tested.

def: A tool that flags stylistic and probable programming errors in source.
ex: The {{code linter}} warned about an unused variable and a missing return.

def: A tool that inspects code for defects without executing it.
ex: The {{static analyzer}} detected a possible null dereference before we shipped.

def: A tool that automatically rewrites source to a consistent style.
ex: The {{code formatter}} reindented the whole file the moment we saved.

def: A tool that verifies values are used consistently with their declared types.
ex: The {{type checker}} rejected passing a string where a number was expected.

def: An agreed set of conventions for writing consistent, readable code.
ex: Our {{style guide}} requires two-space indentation and trailing commas.

def: A script that runs automatically before a commit is finalised.
ex: A {{pre-commit hook}} runs the linter and blocks badly formatted code.

def: A structure showing how modules or packages depend on one another.
ex: The {{dependency graph}} revealed a circular link between two modules.

def: A tool that installs, updates and removes software libraries and their dependencies.
ex: The {{package manager}} downloaded forty dependencies in a single command.

def: A component that selects compatible versions of all required packages.
ex: The {{dependency resolver}} could not satisfy two conflicting version constraints.

def: A file recording the exact dependency versions used for reproducible installs.
ex: Commit the {{lockfile}} so every teammate installs identical package versions.

def: Stored results of previous builds reused to speed up later compilation.
ex: A warm {{build cache}} cut the compile time from minutes to seconds.

def: A tool that automates repetitive build and development commands.
ex: The {{task runner}} rebuilds styles and refreshes the browser on save.

def: A tool that produces source code automatically from templates or specifications.
ex: The {{code generator}} created typed clients directly from the API schema.

def: A tool that installs and switches between multiple runtime versions.
ex: A {{version manager}} lets me switch language runtimes per project instantly.

def: An isolated workspace with its own dependencies separate from the system.
ex: Activate the {{virtual environment}} before installing the project libraries.

def: A restricted space where untrusted code runs without affecting the host.
ex: Run the plugin inside a {{sandbox environment}} so it cannot touch real files.

def: Packaging an application with its dependencies into a portable isolated unit.
ex: {{containerization}} made the app run identically on every developer's laptop.

def: A browser run without a visible window, used for automated testing.
ex: The {{headless browser}} loads each page and screenshots it during the tests.

def: A mode that reruns tests or builds automatically whenever files change.
ex: Leave the tests in {{watch mode}} and they rerun every time you save.

def: Applying code changes to a running app without a full restart.
ex: With {{hot reload}} the button colour updated instantly as I edited.

def: A trial execution that reports actions without actually performing them.
ex: Do a {{dry run}} of the migration first to preview what would change.

def: A rare problem occurring when several conditions reach extremes together.
ex: A nasty {{corner case}} appears only when the list is empty and offline.

def: The default scenario where everything works with valid, expected input.
ex: The demo only covered the {{happy path}} and ignored all error handling.

def: A standard test used to measure and compare performance.
ex: The new algorithm beat the old one on every {{benchmark}} we ran.

def: The practice of systematically measuring performance under controlled conditions.
ex: Careful {{benchmarking}} showed the cache halved the average response time.

def: A file capturing a program's memory state at the moment it crashed.
ex: We opened the {{core dump}} to see which pointer was invalid.

def: A snapshot of all objects in memory used to diagnose leaks.
ex: The {{heap dump}} showed millions of cached strings never released.

def: A snapshot of every thread's state, used to diagnose hangs.
ex: A {{thread dump}} revealed two threads waiting forever on each other.

def: A diagram showing which functions call which others during execution.
ex: The {{call graph}} exposed a surprisingly deep chain of helper functions.

def: A summary document listing which tests passed, failed or were skipped.
ex: The {{test report}} listed three failures with links to their stack traces.

def: A document describing the scope, approach and schedule of testing.
ex: The {{test plan}} outlined which features each release cycle would cover.

def: A stable set of tests rerun to ensure old bugs do not return.
ex: Every fix adds a test to the {{regression suite}} to guard against relapse.

def: A mechanism that decides whether a test's output is correct.
ex: Without a reliable {{test oracle}}, we could not tell right from wrong output.

def: A stored reference output that new results are compared against.
ex: The test failed because output no longer matched the {{golden file}}.

def: Code that prepares state before a test and cleans up afterward.
ex: The {{setup and teardown}} create a temporary folder and delete it later.

def: Running one test repeatedly with different sets of input values.
ex: {{test parameterization}} let a single case cover twenty currency codes.

def: A system for recording, assigning and following defects to resolution.
ex: File the crash in the {{bug tracker}} with steps to reproduce it.

def: A description of a defect including steps, expected and actual behaviour.
ex: A clear {{bug report}} includes the version, the steps and a screenshot.

def: A flaw in software that causes incorrect or unexpected behaviour.
ex: The {{defect}} caused totals to round incorrectly for large orders.

def: Assessing and prioritising reported issues by severity and urgency.
ex: During {{triage}} we marked the login crash as the top priority.

def: Investigating an incident to identify its underlying originating cause.
ex: The {{root cause analysis}} traced the outage to a missing timeout setting.

def: A recorded reference of known-good results used to detect later regressions.
ex: Performance dropped below the {{regression baseline}}, so the build was blocked.

def: A store holding built packages and binaries for reuse and deployment.
ex: The pipeline uploads each release to the {{artifact repository}} automatically.

def: A dedicated machine that compiles code and runs automated tests.
ex: The {{build server}} rejected the branch because two tests failed.

def: Running automated tests continuously throughout the delivery pipeline.
ex: {{continuous testing}} catches broken commits within minutes of pushing.

def: Using tools to run tests automatically instead of manually.
ex: {{test automation}} freed the team from repeating the same checks by hand.

def: A simulated attack that probes a system for security weaknesses.
ex: The {{penetration test}} exposed an unprotected admin endpoint.

def: A configured system that mirrors production for running tests safely.
ex: Deploy to the {{test environment}} before promoting the build to users.

def: A recorded snapshot of program state saved when it crashes.
ex: The {{crash dump}} let us reconstruct the exact call stack at failure.

def: A tool that records tasks, bugs and feature requests for a project.
ex: Every task lives in the {{issue tracker}} with a status and an owner.
